full_response:
We begin with the definition of the inverse metric, where \(g^{\mu\nu}\) is the inverse of \(g_{\mu\nu}\), satisfying

\[
g^{\nu\kappa} g_{\kappa\rho} = \delta^\nu_\rho.
\]

Taking the partial derivative \(\partial_\mu\) of both sides yields

\[
\partial_\mu (g^{\nu\kappa} g_{\kappa\rho}) = \partial_\mu \delta^\nu_\rho = 0.
\]

Applying the product rule,

\[
(\partial_\mu g^{\nu\kappa}) g_{\kappa\rho} + g^{\nu\kappa} (\partial_\mu g_{\kappa\rho}) = 0.
\]

Rearranging,

\[
(\partial_\mu g^{\nu\kappa}) g_{\kappa\rho} = - g^{\nu\kappa} \partial_\mu g_{\kappa\rho}.
\]

Multiplying both sides by \(g^{\rho\lambda}\) (and summing over \(\rho\)),

\[
(\partial_\mu g^{\nu\kappa}) g_{\kappa\rho} g^{\rho\lambda} = - g^{\nu\kappa} g^{\rho\lambda} \partial_\mu g_{\kappa\rho}.
\]

Since \(g_{\kappa\rho} g^{\rho\lambda} = \delta^\lambda_\kappa\), the left-hand side simplifies to

\[
(\partial_\mu g^{\nu\kappa}) \delta^\lambda_\kappa = \partial_\mu g^{\nu\lambda}.
\]

Thus,

\[
\partial_\mu g^{\nu\lambda} = - g^{\nu\kappa} g^{\lambda\rho} \partial_\mu g_{\kappa\rho}.
\]

Renaming indices (\(\lambda \to \rho\) and noting that \(\kappa\) and \(\rho\) are dummy indices) and using the symmetry of the metric (\(g_{\kappa\rho} = g_{\rho\kappa}\)), we obtain

\[
\partial_\mu g^{\nu\rho} = - g^{\nu\kappa} g^{\rho\lambda} \partial_\mu g_{\lambda\kappa}.
\]

\boxed{\partial_\mu g^{\nu\rho} = -g^{\nu\kappa}g^{\rho\lambda}\partial_\mu g_{\lambda\kappa}}

Confidence: 100%
